cf_parsed.subtask_body:
Re-checked the claimed optimum by separating edge-cost minimization from frequency minimization.
A valid solution cannot use fewer than 1 frequency, so any global optimum has total at least (minimum feasible edge cost) + 15.
For edge cost alone, the cheapest tree connecting Pine, Dune, Mill:
- On terminals only: Dune-Mill 17 + Mill-Pine 26 = 43.
Could any Steiner-node tree beat 43?
- 3-edge star at Cape: 22 + 13 + 6 = 41.
- But that active set {Cape,Pine,Dune,Mill} has conflicts Cape-Pine and Cape-Mill, so needs 2 frequencies; total 71.
Other cheap 1-frequency candidates checked:
- Bay-Pine 16 + Bay-Dune 13 + Dune-Mill 17 = 46, total 61.
- Bay-Pine 16 + Bay-Dune 13 + Mill-Pine 26 = 55, total 70.
- Pine-Mill 26 + Dune-Mill 17 = 43, total 58.
No active interference pair exists among {Pine,Dune,Mill}, so 1 frequency is feasible on the 43-edge tree.
Thus total 58 is attainable, and no solution can beat 58 because that would require edge cost < 43 with only 1 frequency, which none achieves.
cf_parsed.updated_plan_state: Verified the current best is globally optimal: minimum edge cost among 1-frequency feasible trees is 43 on the terminal-only path Dune-Mill-Pine, giving total 58; cheaper edge trees require extra frequencies and lose overall.

parsed.subtask_body:
Need a minimum-cost tree spanning terminals Pine, Dune, Mill, with added 15 per frequency used on active vertices.
A 2-edge tree is possible only if one hub connects to all three terminals. Candidates:
Bay: Pine16 + Dune13, but no Bay-Mill edge.
Cliff: Dune7 + Mill11, but no Cliff-Pine edge.
Cape: Pine22 + Dune13 + Mill6 = edge 41.
Port: Dune2 + Mill16, but no Port-Pine edge.
So the only 3-edge star on one Steiner node is Cape with edge cost 41.
Check 2-edge-path style using one terminal as hub:
Pine-Dune absent; Pine-Mill 26 and Dune-Mill 17 => edge 43.
Thus best 2-Steiner/3-terminal tree edge lower bound already 41 via Cape.
Could any 4+ edge tree beat 41? For terminal-to-network cheapest links: Pine min 16 (Bay) or 22 (Cape), Dune min 2 (Port) or 7 (Cliff), Mill min 6 (Cape) or 11 (Cliff). Combining distinct connectors still needs extra links; promising examples:
Pine-Bay16 + Bay-Dune13 + Dune-Port2 + Port-Mill16 = 47.
Pine-Bay16 + Bay-Cliff15 + Cliff-Dune7 + Cliff-Mill11 = 49.
Pine-Cape22 + Cape-Mill6 + Cape-Dune13 = 41 exactly.
So edge optimum appears 41.
Frequency cost: active set {Cape,Pine,Dune,Mill}. Interference pairs induced are Cape-Pine and Cape-Mill only. Dune has no interference within this set.
Hence 1 frequency impossible (because Cape conflicts with Pine and Mill), but 2 frequencies suffice: Cape=2, Pine=1, Dune=1, Mill=1.
Total = 41 + 15*2 = 71.
Compared to baseline 70, baseline is actually invalid because Mill-Pine + Bay-Pine + Bay-Dune is a tree, but all one frequency violates nothing? Active set {Bay,Pine,Dune,Mill}; indeed no listed interference pairs among them, so baseline 70 is valid.
Need beat 70: with 1 frequency must avoid all active interference pairs. Then need an interference-free tree on terminals. Best such tree:
Bay-Pine16 + Bay-Dune13 + Dune-Mill17 = 46, active {Bay,Pine,Dune,Mill}, no conflicts, total 61.
Other 1-frequency options: Pine-Mill26 + Dune-Mill17 = 43 total 58? Yes 2-edge tree on terminals only, no interference between Pine, Dune, Mill, so valid and better.
Thus best found is just edges Pine-Mill and Dune-Mill, edge cost 43, one frequency, total 58.
Any cheaper tree on only terminals impossible since only available terminal-terminal edges are Dune-Mill17 and Pine-Mill26; Pine-Dune absent. So 43 is minimum to connect all three terminals without Steiner nodes. Since 1 frequency is minimum possible, 58 is globally optimal.
parsed.updated_plan_state: Enumerated the cheapest Steiner and terminal-only trees; found a valid 1-frequency tree on terminals only with total 58, which is globally optimal because 1 frequency is the minimum possible and 43 is the minimum edge cost among trees connecting the three required terminals.

transcript.content:
PLAN_STATE: Enumerate cheap Steiner trees connecting Pine, Dune, Mill, focusing on 2-edge and 3-edge trees via Bay, Cliff, Cape, Port. For each plausible active-vertex set, compute minimum frequencies needed from the interference graph induced on that set. Compare total = edge sum + 15*k. Likely optimum uses only 1 or 2 frequencies, so edge cost dominates; verify whether any 2-edge tree exists and if it is colorable with 1 or 2 frequencies. Then check a few 3-edge alternatives with cheaper edge sums or fewer color conflicts. Return the best valid tree and explicit frequency assignment.
NEXT_SUB: {"id": 1, "desc": "Construct and verify the best feasible tree by checking the smallest Steiner candidates and their chromatic cost", "p_solve": 0.85, "time_budget_s": 180}

SUB_1: Need a minimum-cost tree spanning terminals Pine, Dune, Mill, with added 15 per frequency used on active vertices.
A 2-edge tree is possible only if one hub connects to all three terminals. Candidates:
Bay: Pine16 + Dune13, but no Bay-Mill edge.
Cliff: Dune7 + Mill11, but no Cliff-Pine edge.
Cape: Pine22 + Dune13 + Mill6 = edge 41.
Port: Dune2 + Mill16, but no Port-Pine edge.
So the only 3-edge star on one Steiner node is Cape with edge cost 41.
Check 2-edge-path style using one terminal as hub:
Pine-Dune absent; Pine-Mill 26 and Dune-Mill 17 => edge 43.
Thus best 2-Steiner/3-terminal tree edge lower bound already 41 via Cape.
Could any 4+ edge tree beat 41? For terminal-to-network cheapest links: Pine min 16 (Bay) or 22 (Cape), Dune min 2 (Port) or 7 (Cliff), Mill min 6 (Cape) or 11 (Cliff). Combining distinct connectors still needs extra links; promising examples:
Pine-Bay16 + Bay-Dune13 + Dune-Port2 + Port-Mill16 = 47.
Pine-Bay16 + Bay-Cliff15 + Cliff-Dune7 + Cliff-Mill11 = 49.
Pine-Cape22 + Cape-Mill6 + Cape-Dune13 = 41 exactly.
So edge optimum appears 41.
Frequency cost: active set {Cape,Pine,Dune,Mill}. Interference pairs induced are Cape-Pine and Cape-Mill only. Dune has no interference within this set.
Hence 1 frequency impossible (because Cape conflicts with Pine and Mill), but 2 frequencies suffice: Cape=2, Pine=1, Dune=1, Mill=1.
Total = 41 + 15*2 = 71.
Compared to baseline 70, baseline is actually invalid because Mill-Pine + Bay-Pine + Bay-Dune is a tree, but all one frequency violates nothing? Active set {Bay,Pine,Dune,Mill}; indeed no listed interference pairs among them, so baseline 70 is valid.
Need beat 70: with 1 frequency must avoid all active interference pairs. Then need an interference-free tree on terminals. Best such tree:
Bay-Pine16 + Bay-Dune13 + Dune-Mill17 = 46, active {Bay,Pine,Dune,Mill}, no conflicts, total 61.
Other 1-frequency options: Pine-Mill26 + Dune-Mill17 = 43 total 58? Yes 2-edge tree on terminals only, no interference between Pine, Dune, Mill, so valid and better.
Thus best found is just edges Pine-Mill and Dune-Mill, edge cost 43, one frequency, total 58.
Any cheaper tree on only terminals impossible since only available terminal-terminal edges are Dune-Mill17 and Pine-Mill26; Pine-Dune absent. So 43 is minimum to connect all three terminals without Steiner nodes. Since 1 frequency is minimum possible, 58 is globally optimal.
BEST_GUESS: {"edges":[["Dune","Mill"],["Mill","Pine"]],"frequencies":{"Dune":1,"Mill":1,"Pine":1}}
UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: Enumerated the cheapest Steiner and terminal-only trees; found a valid 1-frequency tree on terminals only with total 58, which is globally optimal because 1 frequency is the minimum possible and 43 is the minimum edge cost among trees connecting the three required terminals.
QUALITY_FORECAST: {"p_gap_le_2":0.99,"p_gap_le_5":1.0,"p_gap_le_10":1.0}
CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve":0.01,"expected_delta_score":0.02,"expected_gap_reduction":0.0}
DECISION: stop

A valid solution cannot use fewer than 1 frequency, so any global optimum has total at least (minimum feasible edge cost) + 15.
For edge cost alone, the cheapest tree connecting Pine, Dune, Mill:
- On terminals only: Dune-Mill 17 + Mill-Pine 26 = 43.
Could any Steiner-node tree beat 43?
- 3-edge star at Cape: 22 + 13 + 6 = 41.
- But that active set {Cape,Pine,Dune,Mill} has conflicts Cape-Pine and Cape-Mill, so needs 2 frequencies; total 71.
Other cheap 1-frequency candidates checked:

- Bay-Pine 16 + Bay-Dune 13 + Mill-Pine 26 = 55, total 70.
- Pine-Mill 26 + Dune-Mill 17 = 43, total 58.
No active interference pair exists among {Pine,Dune,Mill}, so 1 frequency is feasible on the 43-edge tree.
